coredata.dc:title: The traditional and modern uses of Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring, in medicine and cosmetic: Applications and bioactive ingredients
coredata.dc:creator: Bailly, Christian
coredata.dc:description:
Ethnopharmacological relevance Extracts of the plant Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring (spike moss) are used for a long time in Asia, for the treatment of multiple diseases and conditions. Aqueous and alcoholic leave extracts are used by local communities. In China, the plant (Juan bai) is listed on the Pharmacopoeia. In South Korea, the use of this plant (Kwon Baek) is mentioned in the book Dongui-Bogam (Heo Jun 1613), at the origin of the Hyungsang medicine. S. tamariscina is traditionally used in Vietnam (mong lung rong), Thailand (dok hin), Philippines (pakong-tulog) and other Asian countries.
Aim of the study To provide an analysis of the multiple traditional and current uses of S. tamariscina extracts (STE) in the field of medicine and cosmetic. The review is also intended at identifying the main natural products at the origin of the many pharmacological properties reported with these extracts (anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antidiabetic, antibacterial, antiallergic, anticancer effects).
Methods Extensive database retrieval, such as SciFinder and PubMed, was performed by using keywords like " Selaginella tamariscina”, “spike moss”, “Selaginellaceae ". Relevant textbooks, patents, reviews, and digital documents were consulted to collate all available scientific literature and to provide a complete science-based survey of the topic.
Results Different solvents and methods are used to prepare STE. The process can largely modify the natural product content and properties of the extracts. STE display a range of pharmacological effects, useful to treat metabolic disorders, several inflammatory diseases and various cancers. A specific carbonized extract (S. tamariscina carbonisatus) has shown hemostatic effects, whereas standard STE can promote blood circulation. Many patented STE-containing cosmetic preparations are reviewed here. Several biflavonoids (chiefly amentoflavone) and phenolic compounds (selaginellin derivatives) are primarily responsible for the observed pharmacological properties. Potent inhibitors of protein tyrosine phosphatase 1 B (PTP1B), phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4), and repressor of pro-inflammatory cytokines expression have been identified from STE.
Conclusion The traditional use of STE supports the research performed with this plant. There are robust experimental data, based on in vitro and in vivo models, documenting the use of STE to treat type 2 diabetes, several inflammatory diseases, and some cancers (in combination with standard chemotherapy). Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) is a prime reservoir for amentoflavone, and many other bioactive natural products. The interest of the plant in medicine and cosmetic is amply justified.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Patented applications and compositions of STE
Pharmacological properties of STE and the active ingredients

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring growing on limestone rocks and its diverse applications. Photos from The Temperate Plants Database (Shipher Wu) (http://temperate.theferns.info/image/Selaginella+tamariscina). The present review is focused on the use of the plant in traditional medicines and cosmetics.

Stir-frying of S. tamariscina to produce the so-called S. tamariscina carbonisatus changes the chemical composition of the plant and hence its coagulation activity. A much lower content in amentoflavone (AMF has anticoagulant activity) was found in the processed product (carbonized) compared to the non-processed plant. In contrast, the content in dihydrocaffeic acid (DHCA has hemostatic activity) was enhanced upon stir-frying (Zhang et al., 2018). Nanoparticules made from S. tamariscina carbonisata (STC) were found to promote coagulation and reduce bleeding time in an animal model (Zhao et al., 2020a).

(A) Pathologies and health conditions that could benefit from the use of S. tamariscina, as claimed in recent patent applications. (b) Cosmetic applications claimed in patents based on the use of products and/or extracts containing S. tamariscina. See text for references to the patents.

Selected compounds isolated from S. tamariscina with an inhibitory activity against Protein Tyrosine Phosphatase 1 B (PTP1B). The molecular model shows a small molecule bound to the active site of PTP1B (PDB access code: 4I8N) (Reddy et al., 2014). Dephosphorylation of the phosphorylated tyrosine residues of insulin receptor (IR) and IR substrates (IRS) by PTP1B negatively regulates the insulin signal transduction. Thus, PTP1B inhibitors enhance insulin signaling.
Structure of various compounds isolated from S. tamariscina.

Anti-inflammatory activity of STE and biflavonoids contained in the extracts. Amentoflavone (AMF), hinokiflavone (HNK), and other biflavones contained in the extracts interfere with the ERK/NFκB signaling pathway to suppress expression and production of inflammatory mediators, in macrophagic cells.

A selection of compounds isolated from S. tamariscina with an inhibitory activity against phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4D2 isoform). The molecular model shows a small molecule bound to the catalytic domain of PDE4D2 (PDB access code: 5WH6) (Feng et al., 2018). PDE4 catalyzes hydrolysis of the second messenger cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) into 5′-AMP. PDE4 inhibitors exert diverse pharmacological effects, including anticancer effects. The indicated IC50 values were taken from the original publications (Liu et al., 2014; Yao et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019; Woo et al., 2019).

row.entry: Rhinitis (including allergic rhinitis), pharyngitis, chronic sinusitis

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: There are several products on the market and their exact composition can vary from one product to another. Some products include the following plants: Euphorbia pulcherrima and Notopterygium incisum.

head.ce:title: The traditional and modern uses of Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring, in medicine and cosmetic: Applications and bioactive ingredients
head.ce:short-title: Medicinal and cosmetic uses of Selaginella tamariscina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extracts of the plant Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring (spike moss) are used for a long time in Asia, for the treatment of multiple diseases and conditions. Aqueous and alcoholic leave extracts are used by local communities. In China, the plant (Juan bai) is listed on the Pharmacopoeia. In South Korea, the use of this plant (Kwon Baek) is mentioned in the book Dongui-Bogam (Heo Jun 1613), at the origin of the Hyungsang medicine. S. tamariscina is traditionally used in Vietnam (mong lung rong), Thailand (dok hin), Philippines (pakong-tulog) and other Asian countries.

To provide an analysis of the multiple traditional and current uses of S. tamariscina extracts (STE) in the field of medicine and cosmetic. The review is also intended at identifying the main natural products at the origin of the many pharmacological properties reported with these extracts (anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antidiabetic, antibacterial, antiallergic, anticancer effects).

Extensive database retrieval, such as SciFinder and PubMed, was performed by using keywords like " Selaginella tamariscina”, “spike moss”, “Selaginellaceae ". Relevant textbooks, patents, reviews, and digital documents were consulted to collate all available scientific literature and to provide a complete science-based survey of the topic.

Different solvents and methods are used to prepare STE. The process can largely modify the natural product content and properties of the extracts. STE display a range of pharmacological effects, useful to treat metabolic disorders, several inflammatory diseases and various cancers. A specific carbonized extract (S. tamariscina carbonisatus) has shown hemostatic effects, whereas standard STE can promote blood circulation. Many patented STE-containing cosmetic preparations are reviewed here. Several biflavonoids (chiefly amentoflavone) and phenolic compounds (selaginellin derivatives) are primarily responsible for the observed pharmacological properties. Potent inhibitors of protein tyrosine phosphatase 1 B (PTP1B), phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4), and repressor of pro-inflammatory cytokines expression have been identified from STE.

The traditional use of STE supports the research performed with this plant. There are robust experimental data, based on in vitro and in vivo models, documenting the use of STE to treat type 2 diabetes, several inflammatory diseases, and some cancers (in combination with standard chemotherapy). Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) is a prime reservoir for amentoflavone, and many other bioactive natural products. The interest of the plant in medicine and cosmetic is amply justified.

ce:section.ce:para: The plant family Selaginellaceae includes only one genus, Selaginella also known as spike moss, which corresponds to a group of early vascular plants with an important place in evolutionary history (Kang et al., 2020). They belong to the Lycopodiophyta division which is the oldest lineage of vascular plants on earth (Weng and Noel, 2013). There are more than 700 Selaginella species distributed worldwide, in a diverse range of habitats, from deserts to tropical rain forests and mountainous regions (Banks, 2009; Shim et al., 2021). These plants contain numerous bioactive natural products, including flavonoids, (neo)lignans, phenols and alkaloids (Almeida et al., 2013). A recent analysis of eight Selaginella species (S. apoda, S. biformis, S. cupressina, S. delicatula, S. erythropus, S. myosuroides, S. uncinate, S. ramosii) revealed the presence of compounds with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and immuno-regulatory properties, as well as a few cytotoxic activities (Křížkovská et al., 2020).

In recent years, significant progresses have been made in the isolation of bioactive products from this family of primitive vascular plants. The species Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) has attracted considerable attention due to its well-established use in traditional medicine and cosmetic. New compounds have been characterized from this species and their pharmacological properties investigated. It is timely to provide an update of the use of the plant, its biological properties and main bioactive constituents. A complete analysis of the published scientific literature and patent coverage on S. tamariscina (P.Beauv.) is presented here.

Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring (synonym: Selaginella tamariscina var. Pulvinata (Hook. & Grev.) Alston; basionym: Stachygynandrum tamariscinum P. Beauv.) is an evergreen perennial plant, hermaphrodite, widely distributed in East Asia. A DNA barcoding method has been developed to precisely identify plants of the family Selaginellaceae (Gu et al., 2013). In English, the plant is often called spike moss (or tamariskoid spikemoss) or little-club-moss. It can grow in the soil or on rocks, generally growing with a densely tufted stem 5–15 cm long, with clustered branches (Fig. 1 ). The plant is commonly found on limestone rocks, at elevations from 100 to 2000 m, though mainly at 500–1500 m. It can be found in a large variety of habitats; the plant being very drought resistant, with numerous fibrous roots.

In most cases, the plant is harvested from the wild for local use as a food and medicine. However, a cultivation method of S. tamariscina has been described in a Chinese patent. After collection of the plant, at any time of the year, the transplantation can be performed within 7 days with a claimed transplanting rate >95 % (CN105340560A). Another Chinese patent describes a technology for the rapid tissue-culture propagation of S. tamariscina based on callus induction (CN107581071A).

The leaves are dense and edible. The young leaves are used as vegetables in the Philippines (Setyawan, 2009). The young shoots are cooked in China. But the edibility rating is considered to be low. However, a method for preparing a healthy food material from steamed S. tamariscina has been reported in a Korean patent (KR20090118399A, 2009). There is also a specific patent on the preparation of S. tamariscina tea bag (KR20140002262U) (Fig. 1).

The species S. tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring is used for a long time in traditional medicine for the treatment of various diseases and conditions, in many Asian countries. In China, S. tamariscina is listed in Chinese Pharmacopoeia and used as a traditional medicine for promoting blood circulation. The plant, known as Juàn bǎi (卷柏), is cited in the famous book “Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing” (The Divine Farmer's Materia Medica) which is one of the foundation books of Chinese medicine, dated from 2737 BCE (Shou-zhong, 1998).

S. tamariscina is a well-known medicinal herb in Korea, known as Kwon Baek or Boo Cheo Son. The Korean book Dongui-Bogam (Dong Yi Bao Jian), literally meaning “a priceless book about medicines of an Eastern Country” (from the royal physician Heo Jun 1613) is one of the most influential texts in Korean medicine, at the origin of the Hyungsang medicine. This book mentions the use of Selaginella (Won et al., 2018). Another widely used prescription book for Korean herbal medicine, Bang-Yak-Hap-Pyun, would also refer to Selaginella (Lee, 2009). The plant is used in many other Asian countries, such as Vietnam (mong lung rong, cay chan vit, thach bachi), Thailand (dok hin, Hek-kting-pek) (Nitta et al., 1980), Philippines (pakong-tulog, pakongcipres), as well as Malaysia (Setyawan, 2009), New Guinea and a few other countries. The whole plant, dried or fresh, can be used to prepare extracts and decoctions. But the leaves are most frequently employed.

The plant is used to treat traumatic and gastro-intestinal bleeding, haematuria, leucorrhoea, haemoptysis in pulmonary disease, and many other health problems including the therapy of advanced cancer. In fact, the traditional medicinal applications are very diverse, ranging from the treatment of jaundice, hepatitis, burns and hemorrhoids, but also prolapse of the rectum, gastro-intestinal hemorrhage, excessive menstrual flow, and others. The medicinal applications of S. tamariscina vary largely from one country to another. The Encyclopedia of Traditional Chinese Medicine (ETCM) has listed more than 200 diseases associated with this herb (Xu et al., 2019a).

In Asia, the dried medicinal plant can be purchased from local specialized market stores, botanical shops or centers for traditional medicines. Several international vendors also propose the plant, mainly in the powdered form. Care should be taken to use the right plant because many Selaginellaceae are similarly shaped and easily confused, which can complicate the correct identification. The dried herb can be used to prepare decoctions, infusions, and various types of extracts, as described below. Alternatively, there are also different ready-to-use TCM products, including the herb S. tamariscina, available on the market. The main three types of products, used to treat rhinitis/sinusitis, arthritis and hemorrhoids in China are indicated in Table 1 . These are complex polyherbal preparations containing S. tamariscina and several other plants, with variations from one marketed product to another. There are probably many other compounds commercialized in China, and in different Asian countries.

S. tamariscina is often used in combination with other plants. For example, the Korean traditional herbal remedy called Pyunkang-tang (or Pyunkang-hwan) is composed of six plants: Adenophora triphilla, Liriope platyphylla, Lonicerae japonica, Rehmannia glutinosa, Selaginella tamariscina and Xantium strumarinum. This preparation is used to manage inflammatory hypersecretion of airway mucus associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), pulmonary fibrosis, pneumonitis, tonsilitis, asthma, or other bronchoalveolar affections. The preparation has shown marked anti-inflammatory effects in different experimental models (Seo et al., 2014, 2016, 2019; Yoon et al., 2020).

Different procedures have been reported to prepare STE. We can define three main types of extracts, depending on the solvent(s) and process used:
-
A water extract, obtained by boiling dried leaves of S. tamariscina (100 g) in distilled water (500 ml) for 30 min. After filtration, the aqueous solution is evaporated under a vacuum and the concentrate is lyophilized. This aqueous extract has revealed anticancer effects, attributed to the presence of small amount of the biflavonoid amentoflavone (AMF) (Kim et al., 2020). However, this compound is sparingly soluble in aqueous buffers. The observed activity may be due to the presence of other flavonoids.
-
An ethanolic STE, prepared by extraction of the dried, minced plant (aerial parts) with 95 % ethanol (3 x 1 L EtOH for 3 h afforded 24.5 g of extract from 200 g of dried plant). In this case, with no doubt, the major constituent was AMF (79 mg/g of STE) (Jung et al., 2017). A similar extract can be prepared using methanol instead of ethanol (Kuo et al., 1998).
-
A chloroform STE, prepared in two steps. The dried plant, frozen and pulverized with liquid nitrogen, is first extracted with 70 % ethanol (1 L for 100 g of dried plant, at room temperature for 3 days). Then distilled water is added to the ethanol fraction (0.5 H2O:1 EtOH volume) and a second extraction is performed with CHCl3 (1:1 vol). After evaporation of CHCl3, a concentrated extract essentially containing AMF can be obtained (Lee et al., 2020).

These three procedures refer to relatively simple, solvent-based extracts used in scientific publications for medicinal research purposes. There are different patents referring to the preparation of S. tamariscina-based products for cosmetic applications. A patent application filed in 2005 described a manufacturing method to prepare an extract of S. tamariscina for cosmetic applications (KR100669362B1, 2005). In this case, an aqueous solution of the plant containing 4–6% acetic acid was heated up to 100–160 °C until the color of the plant leaves turns brown. Then after cooling, a solvent extraction was performed at 70 °C (the patent mentioned a range of solvents, including methanol/ethanol, ethyl acetate, butylene or propylene glycol, dichloromethane, hexane, and others). After filtration and concentration, the filtrate was concentrated. The resulting extract contained phenolic compounds (not specified) with antioxidant properties (KR100669362B1, 2005).

A recent Korean patent specifically describes the preparation and composition of a S. tamariscina extract endowed with antioxidant or antibacterial properties for cosmetic applications. A multistep process is reported to obtain a saturated butanol fraction of S. tamariscina methanol extract as the active ingredient. The final fraction contains different products such as α-linolenic acid, 3-deoxy-d-mannoic acid and guaiacol (KR20190137039A). The type of extract - designated “Buddha extract” in the patent because the authors consider that the plant has “a unique appearance resembling the hand of the Buddha” – show antibacterial and antioxidant effects. It could be used as a cosmetic ingredient to prevent or improve skin diseases such as acne, atopy, eczema, and dermatitis, or to limit skin aging, wrinkles, or loss of elasticity (KR20190137039A). According to the patent, the methanol fraction of this Buddha extract could be used in cosmetic products (lotions, nutritional creams) and various food products.

Another patented process with S. tamariscina consists of considering the plant material as a nutrient for bacterial fermentation. A standard bacterial culture of lactobacillus buchneri, a Gram-positive bacteria producing lactic acid and acetic acid, is inoculated with herba Selaginellae powder (essentially from the plant leaves) to modulate the fermentation process. The method is used to prepare a fermented extract of S. tamariscina which can then be used as a moisturizing agent, or to regulate the skin micro-ecology or other applications in the field of skin-care cosmetics (CN111135123A).

A procedure regularly used in China consists of carbonizing the plant sample to obtain a charcoal-like product, generally used for treatment of haemoptysis, hemorrhage and different types of bleed. The control of haemostasia with charcoal TCM is very ancient and can be traced back to the Han Dynasty. Different carbonizing methods have been developed, the most popular being the stir-frying (Chen et al., 2019a). The charred drug defined as “carbonized Selaginellae herba” or S. tamariscina carbonisatus has shown a marked hemostatic activity by shortening tail-bleeding time, whereas the standard, non-carbonized extract, was inactive in the assays (Zhang et al., 2018). A fractionation has revealed an enrichment in dihydrocaffeic acid (DHCA) in the carbonized extract versus the standard extract, and this compound has hemostatic effect, by promoting platelet aggregation (Fig. 2 ). DHCA is a more potent inhibitor of platelet activation than its phenolic precursor (Baeza et al., 2017). In contrast, the carbonized extract showed a lower content in AMF, which is known to have blood promoting function through a vasodilative effect. In other words, the carbonization process has drastically changed the DHCA/AMF content of the extract and this compositional variation is likely at the origin of the distinctive effects of the standard vs. carbonized extracts on blood coagulation (Zhang et al., 2018). Recently, nanoparticules (with an average size of 1.4–2.8 nm) prepared from S. tamariscina carbonisata were found to significantly reduce the bleeding time in rodent models, by promoting coagulation via activation of the fibrinogen system (Zhao et al., 2020a). Nanoparticules and carbon dots prepared from carbonized plants have revealed similar hemostatic effects (Wang et al., 2018a; Cheng et al., 2019). They are considered for the prevention of hemorrhage-induced liver injury (Zhao et al., 2020b) and other applications (Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Patented applications and compositions of STE
ce:section.ce:para: There is a huge number of patents covering the use of S. tamariscina for multiple applications in the fields of medicine, cosmetic and food. We can categorize the most recent patents as follows:

In the medical domain (Fig. 3 a), there are specific patents about the use of S. tamariscina for the prevention or treatment of (1) acute or chronic degenerative brain diseases (WO2006001664A1, 2006), (2) metabolic syndrome (KR20160116427A, 2016) cardiovascular diseases (KR20050072177A, 2005), (4) cerebrovascular system disease (KR20050072175A, 2005), (5) asthma (KR20130048108A, 2011), (6) pneumonia (KR20130048109A, 2011), (7) atopic dermatitis (KR20130056138A, 2013) cancer (KR20170082812A), (9) coxsackie and herpes virus infections (CN101361765A, 2008) tonsilitis (KR20130042726A, 2011), (10) rhinitis (KR20130048110A, 2011), (11) stomach diseases (CN1640485A), (12) to improve lung function (KR20130056137A, 2013) to control the secretion of cortisol (KR20130079963A, 2013) to enhance activity of the immune system (KR20130081852A, 2013) to treat thrombotic diseases with a pyrrolidinoindoline alkaloid from S. tamariscina (CN104274441A, 2014).

In the cosmetic domain (Fig. 3b), there are also several patents covering the use of different extracts or compositions to protect the skin, to reduce skin aging, or to combat hair loss. For example, a recent patent specifically covers a hair care cleaning composition containing S. tamariscina (CN110897971A). A Korean patent reports the preparation of composition comprising extracts of S. tamariscina (Kwonbaek) and a Saussurea species (Sulolum or suolyeo) for the prevention of skin aging and to reduce skin irritation. The combination is claimed to exert marked anti-inflammatory effects. Anti-oxidative effects and inhibition of matrix metalloproteinase (MMP-1) and prostaglandin biosynthesis in vitro are reported in the patent (KR20050105616A, 2004). There are also patents about combination of S. tamariscina with other plants proposed as cosmetic composition with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects (KR101046524B1, 2008). Importantly, a method has been patented to produce an extract of S. tamariscina with antioxidant and whitening activities for cosmetic applications (KR20170050254A). There are several patents in this field of skin whitening, which is economically important. The combination of S. tamariscina with other plant extracts can reinforce or modulate the whitening activity, and improve skin regeneration (CN107308085A, KR1020200106441A, 2020). There are other patents covering the preparation of different compositions containing S. tamariscina claimed to improve skin conditioning and moisturing, or to treat skin scalds and burns.

The patents mentioned above only represent a fraction of the applications claimed with S. tamariscina. A patent search using the key term S. tamariscina gave more than 3600 references from www.google.com/patents. The large patent diversity reflects the extensive medicinal and economical interest of the plant.

Extracts of S. tamariscina are used in cosmetics (Fig. 3b), as skin conditioning products to take advantage of the regenerating, revitalizing and anti-aging agents contained in those extracts, in particular the phenolic compounds (Lee et al., 2006). One can easily find on the internet complex, multi-ingredient preparations (creams, gels, ointments, serum, lotions, emulsions) used for skin hydration and/or as anti-wrinkle products, which contain S. tamariscina. It is a classical skin care ingredient, used to keep the skin healthy and as a skin regenerator. There are specific STE-based products (such as Gerbex®) recommended for their anti-aging, antioxidant, and/or anti-acne effects. More recently, STE can be found in skin whitening products. Cosmetic products containing STE are used for a long time, but also more sophisticated cosmetic compositions containing purified AMF. There are several patents about novel compositions containing AMF (e.g. TW201836582A, 2018).

The anti-aging effects of STE is entirely plausible. On the one hand, STE have been shown to inhibit expression, secretion, and function of matrix metalloproteinases, notably MMP-2 and MMP-9 in cancer cells (Yang et al., 2013; Hsin et al., 2013) and the expression of MMP-1 in skin fibroblasts (Lee et al., 2008). On the other hands, activation of these MMPs is implicated in epithelial-stromal separation and collagen degradation. Decreasing MMP-2 and MMP-9 expression exerts extracellular matrix-protective effects (Bertolini et al., 2020). Skin creams containing ingredients that downregulate of mRNA expression of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β) and suppress MMP-2/9 expression have been designed as anti-photoaging products (Yun et al., 2019). It is therefore not surprising to see STE in anti-aging skin products. But regrettably, a proper, science-based demonstration of the benefits of these extracts on skin properties has not been published.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pharmacological properties of STE and the active ingredients
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, STE extracts are claimed to be useful for a large number of therapeutic applications, but evidence of activities are not always well established. However, in several fields (discussed below), the medicinal benefit of STE has been well demonstrated and active ingredients have been identified.

One of the main traditional use of S. tamariscina extracts in the Orient is the treatment of hepatitis, and diabetes. This indication is supported by robust experimental data. Early studies had shown that an STE administered to diabetic rats for twelve consecutive days induced a marked decrease of blood sugar and serum lipid peroxide, and an increase in serum insulin level (Miao et al., 1996). Then, it was shown that an ethanol-based STE had a better hypoglycemic effect than an aqueous STE with HepG2 cells. Both extracts could ameliorate the fasting blood glucose in diabetic rats, and they reduced the serum levels of total cholesterol, triglyceride, and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol. The effects were more pronounced with the ethanol extract compared to the water extract (Zheng et al., 2011a). The hypoglycemic effects were attributed to total flavonoid pool of the extract, capable of inducing the expression of peroxisome proliferator activated receptor-γ (PPAR-γ) in adipose tissue and to induce a serum antioxidant effect (Zheng et al., 2011b). The flavonoids efficiently reduced the high blood glucose level, without affecting normal blood glucose level (Zheng et al., 2013a). A recent study has confirmed that a STE, rich in flavonoids, can improve glucose and lipid metabolism of diabetic mice, via an action on the PPAR-γ signaling pathway (Wang et al., 2020).

The use of STE can help to combat obesity, a condition often associated with diabetes. In addition to inhibiting PTP1B, the extract contributes to reduce lipid accumulation in adipocytes and to inhibit adipogenesis and pancreatic lipase activity, at least in vitro (Kim et al., 2019). STE thus shows a dual effect, with anti-diabetic and antiadipogenic activities. The situation is reminiscent to that observed with the PTP1B inhibitor and PPAR-γ agonist vitalboside A, a triterpene glycoside found in Syzygium cumini (jamun) (Thiyagarajan et al., 2016; Sangeetha et al., 2017).

An ethanolic extract (70 %) of S. tamariscina has been shown to inhibit dose-dependently the production of inflammatory mediators (nitric oxide, prostaglandin E2 (PGE2)) and proinflammatory cytokines (interleukins IL-1β and IL-6) in lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophagic cells. The combined anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects of the STE implicated different signaling pathways, mainly the MAPK, NFκB, and Nrf2/HO-1 pathways (Won et al., 2018). This activity can be linked to flavonoid content of the extract.

One of the main chemical characteristics of STE is the high content in biflavonoid molecules, such as AMF which can be found in Selaginellaceae and many other plant families (Yu et al., 2017). STE also generally contain other biflavonoids such as hinokiflavone (HNK) and 7′-O-methyl-hinokiflavone which were both found to exert marked anti-inflammatory effects in LPS-mediated murine macrophages RAW 264.7 and colon epithelial HT-29 cells. These biflavones suppressed the production of the inflammatory mediators NO, IL-6, and IL-8, and tumor-necrosis factor α (TNFα). They also suppressed LPS-induced expression of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and cyclooxygenase (COX)-2, and the activation of nuclear factor κB (NFκB) and extracellular regulated kinases ERK1/2 (Shim et al., 2018) (Fig. 6 ).

STE contain HNK and monomeric flavonoids, such as 6-(2-hydroxy-5-carboxyphenyl)-apigenin (Liu et al., 2010). But the main bioactive compound of STE is AMF, a molecule which displays a large range of pharmacological effects. This compound dose-dependently suppressed the production of NO and PGE2 in cultured macrophages and interacted with ERK (Oh et al., 2013). The anti-inflammatory effects of this dimeric compound have been largely evidenced using diverse experimental models in vitro and in vivo (Ren et al., 2020; Cai et al., 2019; Rong et al., 2019). Notably, AMF is a potent regulator of proinflammatory cytokines in keratinocytes and local skin lesions (An et al., 2016), explaining at least in part, the beneficial effect of STE on psoriasis-like lesions. Other anti-inflammatory and anti-proliferative bioflavonoids have been isolated from STE, such as sumaflavone, selamariscina A, robustaflavone, cupressaflavone, taiwaniaflavone and 2′,8″-biapigenin (Pokharel et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2006; Woo et al., 2006; Park et al., 2020), in addition to a few lignan derivatives which can contribute to the anti-inflammatory effects, such as moellenoside B, liriodendrin and tamariscinosides D-E-F (Dat et al., 2017) (Fig. 5). These different studies support the use of STE for the treatment of diseases with an inflammatory component, such as arthritis and inflammatory bowel disease.

STEs have been traditionally used for the treatment of various types of cancer in China. An ethanolic extract of S. tamariscina (1 g/kg) has revealed a noticeable antitumor activity in a mouse model of lung cancer (A549) equivalent to the effect of the standard cytotoxic drug doxorubicin. The effect was attributed to AMF (tested at 50 mg/kg) which produced almost the same effect as the extract (Lee et al., 2017). AMF exerts a direct anticancer action, with induction of mitochondrial apoptosis (Lee et al., 2011), and inhibits the detoxification enzyme AKR1B10 (human aldo-keto reductase family 1B10) involved in drug resistance (Jung et al., 2017). The antiproliferation and antimetastatic activities of AMF have been largely demonstrated in various experimental models (Kim et al., 2020). The compound has shown anticancer effects in bladder cancer (Chiang et al., 2019), non-small-cell lung cancer (Shen et al., 2019), ovarian cancer (Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2017), osteosarcoma (Pan et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019), glioma/glioblastoma (Yen et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020a), hepatocellular carcinoma (Lee et al., 2018a, 2018b) and other types of cancer. It can potentialize the effects of diverse anticancer drugs, such as platinum derivatives (Hu et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020b) and sorafenib (Tsai et al., 2018). As discussed recently with the related compound HNK, bioflavonoids are useful natural products to improve cancer treatment, but specific oral formulations are often required to improve their solubility, bioavailability and hence their antitumor potency (Chen et al., 2020c; Goossens et al., 2021). HNK is a linear dimer containing two apigenin units linked via an ether linkage (Fig. 5), endowed with significant anticancer properties (Goossens et al., 2021). The two main biflavonoids AMF and HNK can be efficiently obtained from S. tamariscina (using ultrasonic-assisted ionic liquid extraction (Jiang et al., 2020) and they can be encapsulated in various micro/nano-structured polymers (Chen et al., 2020c; Feng et al., 2020).

AMF and HNK are two major compounds which contribute importantly to the anticancer activity, but they are not the sole antiproliferative compounds found in STE. Other polyphenolic molecules have been identified from STE and characterized as anti-metastasis agents, inhibitors of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs). This is the case of selaginellin derivatives, in particular selaginellin H (Fig. 7 ) which has a marked inhibitory effect against MMP-9 (IC50 = 18.3 μM) and antiproliferative effects against cancer cells (Wang et al., 2018b). Selaginellin B found in both S. tamariscina and S. pulvinata has shown also marked anticancer effects, inducing apoptosis and autophagy in pancreatic cancer cells (Chu et al., 2020).

Many natural selaginellins (more than 60) have been isolated from Selaginella species and characterized as anticancer and/or antimicrobial agents (Li et al., 2021). These compounds target the enzyme phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4), which catalyzes the hydrolysis of the ubiquitous second messenger cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and is implicated in various diseases, notably neuro-developmental disorders (Bhat et al., 2020), allergic rhinitis (Janosova et al., 2020), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (Michalski et al., 2012; Peng et al., 2020) and plaque psoriasis (Rapalli et al., 2020). The enzyme is also implicated in cancer, in particular the subtypes PDE4-A and -B which expression has been associated with a poor prognosis in hematologic malignancies (Hsien Lai et al., 2020). PDE4 is now viewed as a potential anticancer target, notably in oncohematology (Cooney and Aguiar, 2016). Various PDE4 inhibitors have been isolated from S. tamariscina, such as the 1H-2-benzopyran derivative selagintamarlin A (IC50 = 40 nM against PDE4D2), derived from selaginellin O (Yao et al., 2017). Other inhibitors of PDE4D2 have been characterized from STE, such as selaginpulvilin derivatives (Fig. 7) and several other less potent compounds (Liu et al., 2014; Yao et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019; Woo et al., 2019). New compounds are regularly isolated from S. tamariscina, such as selagindanone A and 3,4-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)isobenzofuran-1(3H)-one, this latter compound exhibiting a low level of cytotoxicity against human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (Peng et al., 2021) (Fig. 5).

A few other molecules found in STE can contribute to the anticancer effects, such as the triarylbenzophenone named selagibenzophenone B (Fig. 5) which has revealed a moderate activity in vitro against two cancer cell lines (Liu et al., 2020a). The plant also contains a few phytosterols, such as 7β-hydroxycholesterol and 7α/β-hydroxysitosterols, probably implicated in the cytotoxic effects of dichloromethane extracts (Roh et al., 2010). Altogether, these information support the traditional use of STE to treat cancer and to reduce metastasis (Yang et al., 2007). The anticancer activity of STE is well established, as least in rodent experimental models and the product apparently presents a good safety profile. An ethanol extract of S. tamariscina showed no acute toxicity and no hematological toxicity in mice. A sub-acute toxicity study in BALB/c mice revealed that treatment with an ethanolic STE for up to 30 consecutive days at 3 g/kg/day did neither alter the body weights nor the hematological parameters. Moreover, in a single-dose acute toxicity test, an oral administration of the STE at 10 g/kg did not cause any lethality (Le et al., 2012).

The treatment of infections with pathogenic bacteria or fungi is not a very common use of STE, but a 50 % ethanol STE has shown marked antimicrobial activities against oral bacteria, and a synergistic effect when it was combined with antibiotics like ampicillin. The extract proved active against cariogenic bacteria and periodontal pathogens, particularly bacteroides species. Combined with the antibiotic gentimicin, the STE revealed a marked activity against pseudomonas gingivalis (Choi et al., 2019). The activity can be linked to different known antibacterial compounds included in the extract, such as AMF which has antibacterial effects, synergistic with antibiotics (Hwang et al., 2013). But the action can also be linked to other compounds such as the C–O–C-type biflavone isocryptomerin (Fig. 5) which has shown antibacterial effects against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains including clinical isolates of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (Lee et al., 2009a). Like AMF, isocryptomerin exhibits both antibacterial and antifungal activities (Lee et al., 2009b).

AMF has shown activities against several pathogens, including Clostridium perfringens (Liu et al., 2020b), Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Kumar et al., 2019), the zoonotic pathogen Streptococcus suis (Shen et al., 2018), the fungus Candida albicans (Jung et al., 2006, 2007; Hwang et al., 2012) to cite only a few examples. Activities against cyanobacteria (Lee et al., 2020) and antiviral effects have been reported with this multitalented natural product (Lee et al., 2018c; Li et al., 2019). Recently, a marked effect of STE and AMF against gut bacterial β-glucuronidase (GUS) has been evidenced, suggesting that the products can be useful for treating drug-induced enteropathy (Tian et al., 2021).

STE can be useful to treat allergy-related diseases, such as allergic rhinitis, given the capacity of the extracts to reduce the release of vasoactive amines, such as histamine, from mast cells and to weaken their inflammatory action (Dai et al., 2005). These observations are consistent with the use of the afore-mentioned Korean medicine Pyunkang-tang, which contains S. tamariscina and five other plants, for the treatment of allergic asthma (Yoon et al., 2020).

An anti-fatigue effect has been mentioned with polysaccharides extracted from S. tamariscina, but the activity is little documented (Liu and Wang, 2009). A vasorelaxant activity has been reported also with STE. The vascular relaxant effect on rat aortic endothelium was more pronounced with an ethylacetate STE compared to n-butanol or hexane STE and, here again, the effect was associated with the presence of amentoflavone (Yin et al., 2005). AMF can relax vascular smooth muscle via endothelium-dependent nitric oxide-cGMP signaling (Kang et al., 2004) and protects vascular endothelium against oxidative damages (Zheng et al., 2013b).

Among the many traditional applications of S. tamariscina, the treatment of blood-related damages and affections represents a significant part of the prescriptions. The plant is used to treat gastro-intestinal and anal bleedings, haemoptysis (coughing up of blood or blood-stained mucus) associated with pulmonary diseases (lung cancer, pulmonary oedema, tuberculosis, etc). But surprisingly, there are not so many experimental studies to support these types of hemorrhage-associated uses. A marked hemostatic activity has been reported with S. tamariscina carbonisatus which can promote platelet aggregation (Zhang et al., 2018). Nanoparticles derived from this carbonized form of S. tamariscina have shown remarkable hemostatic effects (Zhao et al., 2020a). But apart from these two publications, there is a deficit of studies to support the traditional use of S. tamariscina to treat bleedings. In fact, the use of a standard (non-carbonized) STE to treat bleedings is surprising because the main constituent, AMF, does not directly inhibit aggregation of intact platelets, but it is an inhibitor of human platelet cAMP phosphodiesterase, an enzyme necessary for platelet aggregation (Beretz et al., 1986). Moreover, AMF is an inhibitor of human thrombin (IC50 = 17.8 μM) (Chen et al., 2019b) and it has been proposed, based on an in silico study, that AMF can antagonize the platelet-activating factor (Jasamai et al., 2015). For these reasons, the use of a standard ethanolic extract of S. tamariscina can hardly be recommended to treat pathologies associated with bleedings.

Several plants of the Selaginella family are used for a long time in traditional medicine. This is the case of S. tamariscina which is very popular in China, South Korea, and a few other surrounding Asian countries. The plant is also endemic to India, but in this country the most popular Selaginella species is S. bryopteris, known as “Sanjeevani” (the resurrection herb) in the Indian Ramayana mythology (Chandran and Muralidhara, 2014). These two species, as well as other Selaginella (S. bryopteris, S. involvens, S. kraussiana, S. moellendorffii, S. lepidophylla) are characterized by an extreme desiccation tolerance. S. tamariscina is so resistant to desiccation that it is often considered as a “resurrection plant”. It is an evolutionary adaptation (Agduma and sese, 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Alejo-Jacuinde et al., 2020) and many functional and/or regulatory genes implicated in resistance and reaction to drought conditions have been identified, providing a set of “resurrection genes”, as they are called (Gu et al., 2019). As the consequence, because the plant never seems to die, S. tamariscina (P.Beauv.) Spring is considered to have the property of prolonging life. This type of communication is regularly exploited in the cosmetic field, in addition to promoting the skin conditioning effects of the plant, based on the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory ingredients found in STE.

In the medical domain, the long-established, traditional uses of S. tamariscina is now relatively well supported by experimental data, at least for the treatment of diverse inflammation-related diseases and cancer. But further experimental (and clinical) data would be required to support the use of STE to treat bleeding-associated pathologies and associated symptom. STE, which is anticoagulant, can be used to improve blood circulation but the carbonized form (S. tamariscina carbonisatus), which is hemostatic, is better adapted in cases of bleeding.

S. tamariscina is a rich source of bioflavonoids and an almost limitless source of selaginellin-type compounds. Alcoholic STE, the most frequently used, are chiefly represented by the biflavonoid AMF, which has pleiotropic pharmacological properties. This compound has been even proposed for the treatment of Covid-19 recently (Patil et al., 2021). AMF displays marked anticancer effects and this is consistent with the traditional use of S. tamariscina in cancer, preferably in association with registered drugs. The identification of potent PDE4 inhibitors in STE also led credence to the use of these extracts in cancer and inflammatory diseases.

Extracts of Selaginella tamariscina (P.Beauv.) are used for a long-time in traditional medicine in Asia. Nowadays the plant is teeming with therapeutic and cosmetic applications. The potency of the extracts can be linked to the presence of numerous bioactive ingredients, notably biflavonoids like amentoflavone and selaginellin-type phenolic compounds. The treatment of inflammatory diseases and cancers can benefit from the use of standard alcoholic extracts of native S. tamariscina whereas extracts of the carbonized plant (S. tamariscina carbonisatus) are adapted to treat bleedings. In all cases, the plant contributes to improve Human healthcare. Its many applications can be promoted.
